chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: On my personal validating resolver, I will frequently get  servfail for the initial query of a name (via dig) that then completes successfully if I ask again.  I've assumed it was because the second time had less work to do thanks to the cache

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text:
A lot of managed DNS vendors give per-zone or per-customer unique name server names, presumably in order to allow those zones from attacks or other operational problems.
office.com.     172800  IN  NS  ns1-05.azure-dns.com.
office.com.     172800  IN  NS  ns2-05.azure-dns.net.
office.com.     172800  IN  NS  ns3-05.azure-dns.org.
office.com.     172800  IN  NS  ns4-05.azure-dns.info.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text:
Or... 
microsoft.com.      172800  IN  NS  ns1-39.azure-dns.com.
microsoft.com.      172800  IN  NS  ns2-39.azure-dns.net.
microsoft.com.      172800  IN  NS  ns3-39.azure-dns.org.
microsoft.com.      172800  IN  NS  ns4-39.azure-dns.info.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: You get the idea. :-)

chat.author: Sergey Myasoedov
chat.text: What's the special about X.COM - seem to be a simple resolution?

chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: My server does have a lightly used cache.   I don't personally think that the proper answer is that I ought to be using an external resolver.  (Note, not responding to Shane.   More so to Geoff at the mic. )

chat.author: Tony Li
chat.text: The fact that someone is abusing BGP doesn't mean that the intent has changed. At all.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: The scaling of ASPA impact on routers is going to be ugly.

chat.author: Tom Hill
chat.text: If we let perfect be the enemy of good, there wouldn't be any benefit to ROA/ROV in the wild now -- but it has greatly reduced the accidental hijack problem.

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: The bigger issue for any rpki technology is when providers detect invalid and don’t drop.

chat.text: https://github.com/ietf-wg-idr/RFC4271bis/issues/105

chat.text: Bgp origin issue

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: I wasn't paying attention... where does the ECH information come from when it's not DNS?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: the app client I think

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: Okay, that makes a certain amount of sense, I suppose!

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: See our draft on ECH deployment considerations at https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-campling-ech-deployment-considerations/

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: @Andrew: does your draft call out anything positive about ECH or only negatives? (Will read in a bit)

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: I tihnk I read it before and only saw negative points made but maybe it's changed

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: How do you get a cvss score with network involved that is near 5?

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text:
And when there's not DNS you can get the keying information back from the server when the server does a HelloRetryRequest.
("When a client offers the outer version of an "encrypted_client_hello" extension, the server MAY include an "encrypted_client_hello" extension in its EncryptedExtensions message".  And then that extension contains the list of ECHConfigs with HPKE keys.) 
So that doesn't give you the outer SNI to use, but if you know to use it you can get a key for encrypting the new inner client hello when retrying the clienthello.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: The ECHConfig does include the public_name  which'll be in the 2nd CH  outer SNI in the ECH retry case

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: for a browser anyway

chat.author: Jeffrey Haas
chat.text: Ok 5 is reasonable in some circumstances. Need to see the inputs

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: So... a client can get the ECH info on the first connection and cache it for later use then?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: yep, browsers do that

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: you can do it too with curl but needs manual steps - it's not automatic

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: Cool, thanks!

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: What was the point of that talk?

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: My understanding is that the researchers developed a theoretical model of some trusted computing environment (one used for AI apparently) and found problems with it.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: So, I guess that is the point. :-)

chat.author: Jonas Mücke
chat.text: But clients should ignore the RetryConfigurations if they send a GREASE ECH, i.e., when they do not expect an ECH response.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: I don't recall that as a SHOULD in the ECH RFC but it could be there:-)

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text: So is the Attested TLS protocol that was analyzed fixable as-is, or is it fundamentally broken and needs a different design?  It sounds like "this protocol is broken and doesn't achieve what was intended" which is an interesting discovery and also pushes on the need for formal analysis of cryptographic  protocols before deployment.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: the previous slide was fairly brave in asserting a status for the TLS pure ML-KEM draft;-(

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Rich Salz said:

What was the point of that talk?

That performance is NOT all that matters in operation. When there are HIGH to CRITICAL severity attacks, one needs to consider those.

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text:
The ECHConfig does include the public_name which'll be in the 2nd CH outer SNI in the ECH retry case

But if in the same connection the outer SNI needs to remain the same in the HelloRetryRequest in the same handshake.

chat.author: Michael P
chat.text:
Usama. It's great that we are talking about details of CVEs. 
Can you clarify the scope/scale of what you were discussing?  From the CVE record it looks like an older version of a product, but then you also spoke a bit about unadopted drafts.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: it's not a HRR case, it's a 2nd separate connection for using an ECH retry-config

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: That performance is NOT all that matters in operation. When there are HIGH to CRITICAL severity attacks, one needs to consider those.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Shane Kerr said:

My understanding is that the researchers developed a theoretical model of some trusted computing environment (one used for AI apparently) and found problems with it.

We did develop the theoretical model which helped find vulnerabilities but CVEs are not issued for theoretical vulnerabilities. CVEs are issued for practical implementations. That is to say, the vulnerabilities have been demonstrated on 4 practical deployments of the internet-drafts.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: Sure, fast and insecure is not good.  Did folks appreciate your work and revise their designs?  I think from your last-minuyte summary, it's yes.

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: @Stephen: re our ECH draft, it isn't about whether ECH is good or bad, it simply provides information on the deployment considerations that arise for a set of use cases.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Erik Nygren said:

So is the Attested TLS protocol that was analyzed fixable as-is, or is it fundamentally broken and needs a different design?  It sounds like "this protocol is broken and doesn't achieve what was intended" which is an interesting discovery and also pushes on the need for formal analysis of cryptographic  protocols before deployment.

Please note that four deployments (at least three in production) were analyzed and all of them were shown to be broken. We believe intra-handshake attestation is fundamentally broken and adds unnecessary complexity.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text:
Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

We believe intra-handshake attestation is fundamentally broken and adds unnecessary complexity.

That's a pretty fundamental conclusion.  Are folks still working on it, do they argue with it, or what?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: @andrew: happy to hear that - I had the impression the draft did want to say ECH was bad but good if that's not the case

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Michael P said:

Usama. It's great that we are talking about details of CVEs. 
Can you clarify the scope/scale of what you were discussing?  From the CVE record it looks like an older version of a product, but then you also spoke a bit about unadopted drafts.

I can only talk about the CVE which is public as the follow-up vulnerabilities are under responsible disclosure. We analyzed Cocos AI v0.8.2 (which is intra-handshake attestation) and responsibly disclosed the vulnerability to them. The detailed timeline is here. We proposed Cocos AI to move to post-handshake attestation, and as of 0.9.0, they have moved to post-handshake attestation.

chat.author: Michael P
chat.text: Thanks

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Rich Salz said:

Sure, fast and insecure is not good.  Did folks appreciate your work and revise their designs?  I think from your last-minuyte summary, it's yes.

Yes, as the security advisory mentions our names, Cocos AI acknowledged our work and moved to post-handshake attestation.
CCC acknowledges it but they do not plan to fix their implementation. They are archiving their repo due to vulnerabilities. Edgeless Systems has also applied the mitigations. Meta has moved to a different design, which we believe is still vulnerable.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: @Nalini Elkins my comment was nice to see an IPEG talk where the contents of the packet were something other than DNS.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Rich Salz said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

We believe intra-handshake attestation is fundamentally broken and adds unnecessary complexity.

That's a pretty fundamental conclusion.  Are folks still working on it, do they argue with it, or what?

Maybe not so fundamental as one might think. Most of the industry is using intra-handshake attestation. 
About arguments, I explained the developers acknowledgements above. Here are some arguments of SEAT and UFMRG participants:

https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/ov8f-7cZKK5RZ-Mmjc6IhVSB-Fk/
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/wb_Ys9MZd9u9oM2Bk-8tv7fvXGg/
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/2uUuaD1DygjNDM4GT_-rYbwTiJk/
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/2_aGylmFHoLmqN7BBcYVoH-rNJk/
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/VyifG8zP5aworb_S1NR9FEUEXW0/
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/seat/P_CYTycg0KG7cbKauFA-kVgX2jo/

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: I am around until Saturday and will be very happy to discuss with anyone interested in further details. I am also welcoming more proponents for the proposed Research Group in the IRTF and to scope it according to community interest.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Warren Kumari said:

It seems like discussions on the "Intra-handshake fail " bit should happen in SEAT or TLS..

Yes, discussion is already happening at SEAT, but the vulnerability itself is much wider than SEAT. RATS and TLS ecosystems are both affected, too.

chat.author: Johanna Rooney
chat.text: Apologies about the connection issue.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Joanna: No worries...

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text:
It seems like discussions on the "Intra-handshake fail " bit should happen in SEAT or TLS..

This seems specific to Attested TLS, not regular TLS?  But I think an interesting dimension here if framed as an anecdotal example of "risks of deploying drafts in production in security-sensitive environments prior to security analysis and while they are still drafts" ?

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Due to the technical issues we are adding 3 minutes to the timer.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Erik Nygren said:


It seems like discussions on the "Intra-handshake fail " bit should happen in SEAT or TLS..

This seems specific to Attested TLS, not regular TLS?  But I think an interesting dimension here if framed as an anecdotal example of "risks of deploying drafts in production in security-sensitive environments prior to security analysis and while they are still drafts" ?

Maybe not. It breaks the standard TLS properties, too. It also describes how to (in-)securely apply RATS in practice. One of the drafts, indeed, was proposed for adoption in TLS WG.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: Thank you all for the insightful discussions. Would love to discuss and get feedback during the week.
